title: Cat Facial Swelling: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your cat's face or jawline swollen? Learn about dental abscesses, bites, and why you should contact a vet within 24-48 hours.
content_html:
TL;DR: Facial swelling in cats is often caused by dental issues, infections, or stings and should be evaluated by a vet within 24–48 hours, unless your cat has difficulty breathing, which requires immediate emergency care.

What causes facial or jaw swelling in my cat?

Swelling on a cat's face or jawline is typically a localized inflammatory response. Common causes include dental abscesses, infected bite wounds from other animals, insect stings, or in some cases, tumors. Because cats are prone to hiding discomfort, a visible lump is often the first sign of a deeper infection.

Is my cat's facial swelling a medical emergency?

  • Facial swelling is generally a medium urgency situation that should be addressed by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours.
  • It becomes an immediate emergency if accompanied by difficulty breathing or a blue tint to the gums.
  • Severe lethargy may indicate an anaphylactic reaction or a blocked airway, requiring instant intervention.

How does a photo help my veterinarian triage facial swelling?

  • A high-quality image allows the vet to see the exact location of the swelling and identify possible puncture wounds.
  • Visual information helps the vet assess active drainage or skin discoloration.
  • A photo helps determine if your cat needs an urgent squeeze-in appointment or if it can wait for a standard scheduled visit.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Facial swelling in cats can be attributed to various causes, including an inappropriate inflammatory response to antigens, with a high percentage of affected cats being chronic carriers of feline calicivirus. Severe ulceroproliferative inflammation involving the caudal oral cavity and the area at and lateral to the palatoglossal folds bilaterally is pathognomonic for feline stomatitis (FS). Diagnosis is made by visual identification of bilateral inflammation in the caudal oral cavity during oral examination; advanced cases may show strong objection to opening the mouth. Additional diagnostics include virus isolation (e.g., calicivirus and herpesvirus), retroviral tests, and evaluation for systemic disease. In atypical cases with unilateral involvement or proliferative focal lesions, biopsy and histopathologic evaluation are required to exclude oral neoplasia or other oral disorders.

Chapter: Dentistry, Infectious Disease, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 362)

faqs.question: Can a dental issue cause a cat's face to swell?
faqs.answer: Yes, an infected tooth root or a dental abscess is one of the most common reasons for swelling under the eye or along the jawline in cats.

faqs.question: Should I apply a warm compress to the swelling?
faqs.answer: A warm compress may help if the swelling is an abscess, but only if your cat tolerates it. Do not force it, and avoid using hot water which can burn their sensitive skin.

faqs.question: Could an insect bite be the cause?
faqs.answer: Yes, bee stings or spider bites often cause sudden facial swelling. If you suspect an allergic reaction, monitor their breathing closely and contact a vet.
